questions.text: Choose the correct indirect speech:He said to me, “traitor”.
questions.subject: English

options.text: He said to me that I was a traitor.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it fails to capture the direct accusation or naming implied in the original speech. The verb 'called' is more appropriate for directly addressing someone as something.

options.text: He told me that I have been a traitor.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect as it changes the tense and alters the meaning. 'Have been' suggests a completed action in the past, which does not match the tone or context of the direct speech.

options.text: He called me a traitor.
options.explanation: This option is correct. The verb 'called' is suitable for reporting someone being directly addressed by a specific term. It captures the essence and tone of the original direct speech.

options.text: He exclaimed with anger that I was a traitor.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Although it adds emotional context, it inaccurately reflects the original speech's simplicity. The term 'called' is more straightforward and precise for this context.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is: He called me a traitor. In this case, the direct speech 'He said to me, "traitor"' involves the speaker directly addressing the listener with a specific term, 'traitor.' When converting such expressions to indirect speech, the verb 'called' is most appropriate as it conveys the act of naming or labeling someone. The other options either alter the tense, add unnecessary emotional context, or do not accurately reflect the original tone and intent of the direct speech.

questions.text: Choose the correct indirect speech:He said, "What is the matter"?
questions.subject: English

options.text: He said what the matter was.
options.explanation: Option (a) is incorrect because it uses "said" instead of "asked," which does not appropriately capture the questioning nature of the original statement.

options.text: He asked what the matter was
options.explanation: Option (b), "He asked what the matter was," is the correct choice. This option correctly conveys the original question in indirect speech, changing "is" to "was" and properly using "asked" to indicate that it was a question

options.text: He enquired that what was the matter
options.explanation: Option (c) is incorrect because it awkwardly includes "that" and changes the structure unnecessarily, which is not standard for indirect speech

options.text: He asked that what the matter had been
options.explanation: Option (d) is incorrect because it uses "had been," which is an unnecessary change to the past perfect tense and also misrepresents the time frame of the original question

questions.explanation: Option (b), "He asked what the matter was," is the correct choice. This option correctly conveys the original question in indirect speech, changing "is" to "was" and properly using "asked" to indicate that it was a question.

questions.text: He said. ''you need not wait". Choose the correct indirect speech:
questions.subject: English

options.text: He said that I need not wait.
options.explanation: This option is correct. In the direct speech, "He said" is the reporting verb indicating that someone is conveying information or a statement. The subject "He" remains the same in indirect speech, but the verb "said" changes according to the context and tense. The direct statement "you need not wait" is directly quoted but needs to be transformed into reported speech. In indirect speech, the pronoun "you" changes according to who is reporting the statement. Here, it changes to "I" because it's now the narrator reporting what was said. The verb "need" remains the same.

options.text: He said you needed no wait.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect.“Needed no wait” is wrong because “need” must be followed by a noun or “to + verb,” and “wait” here is a verb.The correct structure is “didn’t need to + verb,” so we say “didn’t need to wait.”

options.text: He said that wait was not need by you
options.explanation: This option is incorrect.You need “was not needed” because “need” in passive becomes needed.The noun “wait” requires an article (“the wait”) to make the sentence grammatically complete.

options.text: He said that you must not wait
options.explanation: This option is incorrect.The sentence “He said that you must not wait” is grammatically correct and clearly expresses prohibition. “Must not” shows strong instruction or warning, meaning you are not allowed or should not wait

questions.explanation: In the direct speech, "He said" is the reporting verb indicating that someone is conveying information or a statement. The subject "He" remains the same in indirect speech, but the verb "said" changes according to the context and tense. The direct statement "you need not wait" is directly quoted but needs to be transformed into reported speech. In indirect speech, the pronoun "you" changes according to who is reporting the statement. Here, it changes to "I" because it's now the narrator reporting what was said. The verb "need" remains the same.

questions.text: Anum asked me, "Did you see the drama on television last night?" Choose the correct indirect speech:
questions.subject: English

options.text: Anum asked me whether I saw the drama on television the earlier night.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Reported speech requires past perfect (“had seen”) when the original question is in the past.The earlier night” is acceptable but “the previous night” is more natural in English.

options.text: Anum asked me whether I had seen the drama on television the earlier night.
options.explanation: Interrogative sentences beginning with an auxiliary verb are changed into indirect speech using the connective if or whether. The reporting verb said (or any other word used as the reporting verb) changes to asked, queried, questioned, demanded of, or enquired of in the indirect speech. Note that of is used after enquired and demanded only when the reporting verb has an object.

options.text: Anum asked me did I see the drama on television the last night.
options.explanation: 1. In reported speech, you do not use the question form “did I see”; instead you use the statement form “I had seen.”2. “Last night” is fine, but after reporting, we often use “the previous night.”

options.text: Anum asked me whether I had seen the drama on television the last night.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect.The sentence is almost correct, but in reported speech, “the last night” is unnatural; it should be “the previous night.”. Using “had seen” is correct because reported speech shifts the tense back from past simple to past perfect.

questions.explanation: Interrogative sentences beginning with an auxiliary verb are changed into indirect speech using the connective if or whether. The reporting verb said (or any other word used as the reporting verb) changes to asked, queried, questioned, demanded of, or enquired of in the indirect speech. Note that of is used after enquired and demanded only when the reporting verb has an object. So B will be the correct option.

questions.text: Choose the correct indirect speech:She said, “What a lovely dress it is.”
questions.subject: English

options.text: She exclaimed that it is a lovely dress.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the verb 'is' should be changed to the past tense 'was' to match the rules of indirect speech conversion.

options.text: She exclaimed that it was a lovely dress.
options.explanation: This is the correct option because it properly converts the direct speech into indirect speech by changing the verb 'is' to 'was' and restructuring the sentence as an exclamatory statement.

options.text: She exclaimed that what a lovely dress it was.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it retains the exclamatory form 'what a lovely dress' which should be restructured in indirect speech.

options.text: She exclaimed what a lovely dress it is.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect as it fails to convert the verb 'is' to the past tense 'was', and it also retains the exclamatory form.

questions.explanation: In indirect speech, the tense of the verb usually shifts back to reflect the fact that the reporting is happening after the original speech. As a result, 'is' becomes 'was'. Additionally, exclamatory sentences are often reported by using verbs like 'exclaimed' and converting the sentence into a statement. The correct answer, Option B, successfully makes these adjustments. Option A fails to change the verb tense. Option C improperly retains the exclamatory form. Option D also fails to change the verb tense and retains the exclamatory structure.

questions.text: Choose the correct indirect speech:The teacher said, “Amna, watch your steps.”
questions.subject: English

options.text: The teacher ordered Amna that she should watch her steps.
options.explanation: This option incorrectly uses 'that she should', which is not necessary in this context. The correct form is to use 'to' followed by the base form of the verb.

options.text: The teacher ordered Amna to watch your steps.
options.explanation: This option incorrectly uses 'your', which should be changed to 'her' to match Amna as the subject of the sentence.

options.text: The teacher ordered Amna to watch her steps.
options.explanation: This option correctly changes the direct speech into indirect speech by using 'ordered' to reflect the teacher's intention, and 'her' as the correct possessive adjective for Amna.

options.text: The teacher requested Amna to watch your steps.
options.explanation: This option incorrectly uses 'requested' instead of 'ordered'. The context suggests an order, not a request, and also uses 'your' instead of 'her'.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is: 'The teacher ordered Amna to watch her steps.' In indirect speech, the verb 'said' is typically changed to match the speaker's intention—in this case, 'ordered' is appropriate as the teacher is giving an instruction. Additionally, the possessive adjective should match the subject (Amna), so 'her' is used instead of 'your'. Other options either use incorrect pronouns or verbs that do not accurately reflect the speaker's intention.

questions.text: Aneela Said, "Shahana is not doing her work properly".Convert to Indirect Speech
questions.subject: English

options.text: Aneela say that Shahana dia ot do her work properly.
options.explanation: There are spelling errors.

options.text: Aneela said that Shahana was not doing her work properly.
options.explanation: According to the rules of converting a direct speech into indirect speech; if the reported speech has a past verb e.g., "said" in the given sentence, the reporting speech should also be change to past tense. Also we place "that" before the reporting speech. The remaining sentence remains same. Therefore, option B is correct.

options.text: Shahana did not do her work properly was said by Aneela.
options.explanation: According to the rules of converting a direct speech into indirect speech; if the reported speech has a past verb e.g., "said" in the given sentence, the reporting speech should also be change to past tense. Also we place "that" before the reporting speech. The remaining sentence remains same. Since the order of sentence is changed; therefore, option C is incorrect.

options.text: She is not doing her work properly is what Aneela said about Shahana.
options.explanation: According to the rules of converting a direct speech into indirect speech; if the reported speech has a past verb e.g., "said" in the given sentence, the reporting speech should also be change to past tense. Also we place "that" before the reporting speech. The remaining sentence remains same. Since the order of sentence is changed; therefore, option D is incorrect.

questions.explanation: According to the rules of converting a direct speech into indirect speech; if the reported speech has a past verb e.g., "said" in the given sentence, the reporting speech should also be change to past tense. Also we place "that" before the reporting speech. The remaining sentence remains same. Since, in options C and D, the order of sentence is changed; therefore, they are incorrect. The option A is incorrect because of spelling mistakes.

questions.text: I said to you,"What a nice scenery!"(Choose the correct indirect narration for the given sentence.)
questions.subject: English

options.text: I exclaimed that it was a nice scenery.
options.explanation: Option A is the correct option since exclaimed is used instead of told, and reported speech is changed to past tense.

options.text: I exclaimed that it is a nice scenery.
options.explanation: Option B is incorrect because reported speech is not converted to past tense.

options.text: I told you that what a nice scenery.
options.explanation: Option C is incorrect because of the use of told and the absence of a helping verb in the reported speech.

options.text: I told you that what was a nice scenery.
options.explanation: Option D is incorrect because of the use of told instead of exclaimed.

questions.explanation: To make an indirect narration of a sentence with an exclamation mark, change said to exclaimed.In other words, an exclamatory sentence becomes an assertive sentence.

questions.text: Identify the correct indirect form for the sentence given below:The speaker said to the audience, "Will you listen to me?"
questions.subject: English

options.text: The speaker asked the audience if they had listened to him.
options.explanation: This option incorrectly uses "had listened," which implies the action was completed in the past. The original question is in the future tense, so "had listened" does not fit.

options.text: The speaker asked the audience if they will listen to him.
options.explanation: This option uses "will listen," which is incorrect in reported speech. In indirect speech, "will" changes to "would" to reflect the shift in tense.

options.text: The speaker asked the audience if they would listen to him.
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. "Will" changes to "would" in indirect speech to match the past tense of "said," and "if" introduces the yes/no question form.

options.text: The speaker asked the audience to listen to him.
options.explanation: This option changes the meaning of the original sentence. It suggests a request rather than a question, so it does not convey the speaker’s actual inquiry.

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. "Will" changes to "would" in indirect speech to match the past tense of "said," and "if" introduces the yes/no question form.
